prompt: Give the root and pattern of كَاتِب and what the pattern names.
expected_answer: Root ك ت ب (writing) in pattern فَاعِل = writer / one who writes (ism fāʿil, the active participle). The long ā after the first radical plus kasra on the second is the agent signature.
accepted_variants: root ك ت ب, pattern فَاعِل, ism fāʿil writer
فاعل = the active participle, one who does the act
required_reasoning: class named as active participle not verb

concept: same root, different pattern, different word
prompt: Give the root and pattern of كِتَاب and why it differs from كَاتِب.
expected_answer: Root ك ت ب in pattern فِعَال = a book. The same three radicals as كَاتِب in a different pattern give a different word: the root is the theme (writing), the pattern is the thing or role.
accepted_variants: root ك ت ب, pattern فِعَال, a book
same radicals, different pattern, different word
forbidden_answers: it is the same word as كَاتِب

prompt: Give the root and pattern of مَكْتُوب and its signature.
expected_answer: Root ك ت ب in مَفْعُول = (something) written (ism mafʿūl, the passive participle). The مـ prefix plus long ū before the last radical is the patient signature.
accepted_variants: root ك ت ب, pattern مَفْعُول, passive participle written
ism mafʿūl, the مـ + long ū signature
forbidden_answers: it is the same as the verb
required_reasoning: class not glossed as the bare verb

concept: tense is in the pattern, not the root
prompt: For يَكْتُبُ vs كَتَبَ, name each pattern and which carries the tense.
expected_answer: Root ك ت ب. كَتَبَ = فَعَلَ (Form I past, he wrote); يَكْتُبُ = يَفْعُلُ (Form I present, he writes). The يـ prefix plus the vowel pattern marks present tense, so the pattern carries the tense, not the root.
accepted_variants: كَتَبَ past فَعَلَ, يَكْتُبُ present يَفْعُلُ; pattern carries tense
the يـ prefix + vowels mark present, not the root
forbidden_answers: tense is not readable from the form
required_reasoning: tense attributed to pattern not root

concept: doubled middle radical (shadda) marks Form II
prompt: Give the root, pattern, and sense of عَلَّمَ, and what the shadda signals.
expected_answer: Root ع ل م (knowledge) in فَعَّلَ (Form II) = he taught (made-to-know, causative). The shadda on the middle radical is the whole signal of Form II; do not read it as an extra root letter. Form I عَلِمَ = he knew.
accepted_variants: root ع ل م, Form II فَعَّلَ, he taught (causative)
shadda on middle radical = Form II, not a third radical
forbidden_answers: the shadda is an extra root letter
the doubled letter is a geminate root
required_reasoning: Form II sense distinguished from Form I

concept: place-noun مـ prefix is not a radical
prompt: Give the root and pattern of مَسْجِد and what the leading مـ is.
expected_answer: Root س ج د (prostration) in pattern مَفْعِل = a place of prostration / mosque (ism makān). The leading مـ is the place-noun prefix, not a radical; strip it before reading the root.
accepted_variants: root س ج د, pattern مَفْعِل, place-noun mosque
strip the مـ place-noun prefix before the root
forbidden_answers: the مـ is the first radical
required_reasoning: مـ prefix stripped before root extraction
root extracted as س ج د

concept: tāʾ marbūṭa marks gender, not a radical
prompt: Give the root of مُسْلِمَة and what the ة marks.
expected_answer: Root س ل م (submission/peace) in مُفْعِل + ة = a Muslim woman. The ة is the feminine ending; the masculine is مُسْلِم. The ة never counts as a root letter.
accepted_variants: root س ل م; ة is the feminine ending, not a radical
مُسْلِمَة = feminine of مُسْلِم
forbidden_answers: root is س ل م ة

prompt: Classify مُسْلِمُونَ and رِجَال as sound or broken plural and say how each is formed.
expected_answer: مُسْلِمُونَ is a sound masculine plural (singular plus ـُونَ suffix, root س ل م untouched). رِجَال is a broken plural (root ر ج ل re-poured into pattern فِعَال, the singular رَجُل reshaped, not suffixed) = men.
accepted_variants: مُسْلِمُونَ sound (suffix ـُونَ), رِجَال broken (reshaped فِعَال)
one is suffixed, the other re-patterned
forbidden_answers: both are the same kind of plural
the root changes in a sound plural
required_reasoning: sound plural identified by the suffix
singular kept distinct from plural shape

concept: the maṣdar (verbal noun) is a noun
prompt: Give the root and pattern of إِسْلَام and its class.
expected_answer: Root س ل م in the Form IV maṣdar pattern إِفْعَال = submission / Islam. It is a noun (the act of submitting), so gloss it as a noun, never to submit. Class first, then meaning.
accepted_variants: root س ل م, Form IV maṣdar إِفْعَال, a noun
maṣdar = verbal noun, gloss as a noun not the verb
required_reasoning: class fixed as noun before glossing

concept: a weak middle radical hides in the surface
prompt: Give the root of قَالَ, name its type, and explain the alif.
expected_answer: Root ق و ل, a hollow root with middle radical و, Form I past. The و surfaces as a long ا in قَالَ and returns as و in يَقُولُ / قَوْل. The alif is the weak middle radical, not a stray long vowel.
accepted_variants: root ق و ل, hollow; the alif is the و radical surfacing
weak middle radical و appears as ا in قَالَ
forbidden_answers: the alif is a stray long vowel
required_reasoning: weak radical identified in the middle position
alif attributed to the weak radical

concept: shadda from a geminate root, not Form II
prompt: Give the root of رَدَّ and say why its shadda is not Form II.
expected_answer: Root ر د د (last two radicals identical), shown contracted with a shadda: رَدَّ = he returned (it). The shadda here is two identical radicals merged, not Form II. Tell them apart by whether the doubled letter is the middle radical (Form II) or the last (geminate).
accepted_variants: root ر د د, geminate; shadda = merged last radicals not Form II
doubled LAST radical = geminate, doubled middle = Form II
forbidden_answers: the shadda is a derived-form marker here
required_reasoning: shadda attributed to identical last radicals

concept: hamza as a full first radical
prompt: Give the root of أَمَرَ and explain the hamza.
expected_answer: Root أ م ر (command); the hamza is the first radical, a full consonant. أَمَرَ = he commanded (Form I). Do not drop the hamza when recording the root, and never let norm() erase its seat.
accepted_variants: root أ م ر; hamza is the first radical, keep its seat
do not drop the hamza radical or its seat
forbidden_answers: the hamza is a vowel mark
the hamza can be dropped from the root
required_reasoning: hamza identified as a consonant radical

concept: do not mis-split tanwīn fatḥ alif as a pronoun clitic
prompt: Before extracting a root, why must تَنْوِين fatḥ alif ـًا not be read as the pronoun نا?
expected_answer: A visible token may be host plus a particle or pronoun, but the tanwīn fatḥ alif ـًا is a case ending, not the clitic نا. Reading it as a pronoun is a false clitic split that invents an attached word and corrupts the host. Account for visible pieces without inventing one.
accepted_variants: ـًا is tanwīn fatḥ alif (a case ending), not the pronoun نا
do not invent a نا clitic from the tanwīn alif
forbidden_answers: ـًا is always the pronoun نا
every final alif is a clitic
split off نا whenever you see a final alif
required_reasoning: visible pieces accounted for without inventing one
tanwīn fatḥ alif distinguished from the نا clitic

concept: qamustyping4 visible derivative prefix and plural suffix
prompt: For ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ, name the visible morphology pieces a rich hover must teach.
expected_answer: The token contains the article ٱلْ, the derivative/participle prefix مُ, the host بْطِلُ from ب ط ل, and the masculine plural suffix ونَ. A learner-ready hover may be compact, but it must show the article, derivative prefix, host, and plural ending rather than one undifferentiated host span.
accepted_variants: article + مُ derivative prefix + بطل host + ون plural suffix
ٱلْ + مُ + بْطِلُ + ونَ, active participle plural
